comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's a little past midnight as I type this, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. This week's classic book of Western occultism is The Middle Pillar by Israel Regardie. Regardie's a major presence in modern occult literature, and one of his other books has been featured in an upcoming Magic Monday, but this one also deserves a place; it's the book that introduced the most widely practiced system of internal energy work in modern ceremonial magic, and has earned a place as a classic. (For best results, read it along with Regardie's fine little essay The Art of True Healing and Dion Fortune's The Circuit of Force.) 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you next Monday!***

comments.content:
1. I’m currently working my way through the AODA first degree, with the goal of joining the GCC and eventually becoming a bishop. (I find it interesting there seem to be a few people doing it! I hope it’s a good sign for the future of the order) I’ve decided to try to get as far as possible with it now, and so I decided to look at what requirements of the later degrees can be knocked out now, and start on them. So, for example, I’m putting a lot more than the minimum time into my bardic exploration, so as to be able to use it a few years down the road for the third degree project.

I’m now wondering how this relates to the “will only one thing” you discussed last week. While I suspect I could get a few good meditations out of it, here’s what I’m currently thinking: my will is focused on “Become a bishop in the GCC”, and the steps getting me there are in a way less important than the goal. For example, I’m meditating to bring that future into existence, not for the sake of meditation itself.

Currently my will is focused on bringing that future into manifestation, and so that requires certain concrete steps, but I’m taking them with the big picture goal in mind. This implies there’s at least two types of will: will to do, and will to bring forth; an active and a visionary form. If you lack the active form, you end up with provisional living, and if you lack the visionary, you end up drifting through life, always doing something, but never accomplishing anything.

Do you think this analysis makes sense? Is there anything you’d add to it?

2. For the AODA earth path, I’ve decided to make two super simple changes so as to spare more energy for the third one. I have a very unhealthy relationship with the internet, and want to change that. I suspect part of it is that I have no idea what life was like prior to the existence of the internet, since I’ve had home internet for literally my entire life.

I’ve since realized that my goal is a negative one: cutting internet, and so it reinforces the appeal of the thing. I reframed it as “going analog”, which has helped a great deal, but I’m still unable to craft a simple, clear, and coherent positive goal.

a) Am I correct to think that positive goals are much better than negative ones?

b) Do you have advice on how to craft such goals?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It's entirely appropriate to have one primary focus of will, and have other secondary purposes unfold from that. Someone who wants to become filthy rich, for example, will have a long series of secondary goals subordinate to that desire: cutting useless expenditures, getting a job with a higher salary, excelling at that job, being promoted, etc., etc. You're pursuing a saner goal, but the principle is the same: the goal of walking to a distant place is broken up into individual footsteps, each of which has to be taken in turn. Your differentiation between active and visionary will is sometimes seen as the distinction between will and imagination; one way or another, though, it's valid.

2a) Yee.

2b) Focus on what you want to draw to you. Why are you cutting your internet use? What will you do with the time you free up? What benefits do you expect to gain? Make those the center of your intent.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Do you know good sources which discuss will and imagination?

2b) This raises three questions for me:

i) I have no idea what life was like before the internet: I've had access to it at home since the day I was born. I'm not sure what life without it, or even just in a sane relationship with it, will look like. What I know is I don't like how much I use it.

In this case, am I correct to think it makes sense to focus on one aspect of it at a time, while I figure out what my positive goal overall is? For example, starting out with the goal of getting better sleep by not using the internet right before bed?

ii) Am I correct to think journaling could be useful for figuring out what positive the goal I aim for should be?

iii) Do you think the fact that contemporary American culture is flooded with negative goals ("Don't eat this; don't smoke; stop drinking; etc) is part of why so many people flounder about, unable to achieve much of anything?

comments.content: Do you have any idea what the ever living frack is going on with the over the top reaction to the coronavirus?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There are several things involved. First, the corporate media is desperate for clickbait -- a lot of mainstream media outlets, CNN and MSNBC among them, have suffered disastrous declines in viewership over the last few years and they're facing a serious loss of ad revenue, and whipping up a panic is one way to improve their stats. Second, the Boomer generation has become increasingly frantic about the approaching statistical end of its time on this planet, and this epidemic -- which is primarily lethal to the elderly -- has suddenly brought home to them the hard reality that no amount of botox will keep the Reaper at bay; thus a lot of them are coming loudly unglued. Third, we've got a very high level of social stress just now, with the cage match between the establishment gerontocracy and the populist insurgency rising toward its peak, and people are jittery -- thus anything shocking will tend to make them wig out. Combine those three factors with the ordinary human response to a pandemic and I think that pretty much accounts for it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(Not the OP). Hm, that would go some way to explaining the phenomenon, but not totally. Basically, governments and the media have decided that this virus is bad enough that they need to collectively shoot themselves in the foot by tanking their economies to prevent the spread of it. If the virus isn't that bad, then why would they inflict such economic damage to themselves?

OK, I take your point that death is a pretty good motivator, but I wonder if people everywhere are so fearful because they know deep down how fragile the food and health-care systems are - that hospitals don't have the resources or space to cope with a sudden influx of patients, for example, as already seen in northern Italy.

Also: equally as many people are worried about the economic fallout, so there's a feedback loop between anxiety at falling stock markets and anxiety at the spread of the virus (see the price of Bitcoin? So much for cryptocurrencies being a safe haven, which is why I've stayed far away from them).

Lastly, I think that wealthier and middle class people in the West have had no experience of real hardship for the most part, and suddenly everything they've seen in Hollywood movies such as Contagion and Outbreak are coming to pass, and they're going bonkers.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Governments aren't shooting themselves in the foot -- they're taking advantage of an exogenous crisis to deflate some dangerously overinflated markets. Had the markets just kept on zooming upwards until the bubbles popped, a lot of national leaders would have been blamed for the consequences -- and in particular, that could have messed over Trump's chances at reelection. Instead, the virus will be blamed for the crash, and once the outbreak is over -- a matter of a couple of months, to judge from China and Korea -- markets will pop back up again and, memories being short, all will be well.

But you're right, of course, that people have watched way too many pandemic-apocalypse movies -- and that too many of them have no experience of actual hardship, much less something that could, you know, actually kill them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To add to my earlier comments, here's a half-formed thought: the other thing potentially at work is perhaps that wealthier and middle class people realize somewhere that their lifestyle has helped spread the coronavirus - it's basically been spread by air travel. In the same way that a good horror movie evokes fear by twisting some aspect of culture to expose something dark in it, the spread of this virus evokes fear because it's showing that air travellers are helping to spread it. The virus has taken something our culture celebrates, the freedom of air travel, and turned it into something to fear.

Also, and this might be pushing it, the current conflict between globalists and localists is at play here too. Those wealthy and middle class air travellers who don't support Trump and his protectionist policies (and that's a lot of them) are suddenly confronted with a virus which is shutting down borders. I can't put my finger on it all yet, though...

replies.content:
This is Joe Rogan interviewing Michael Osterholm on a wide range of infectious disease topics, starting with Coronavirus, of course, but also Lyme Disease, Chronic Wasting Disease, etc. You can get at least 90% of the program just from the audio, so give it a listen.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E3URhJx0NSw

Just now, he's explained that Native Americans traditionally burned over northern forests, which helped reduce mice and ticks, but with fire suppression, deer are carrying ticks into moose habitat, and the ticks are killing the moose (both by disease transmission and simply blood loss). It's great thread of systems thinking.

A noteworthy comment on hand-washing (from the program above): it doesn't hurt, but most COVID-19 transmission is airborne. But you can DO hand-washing, and you can't stop breathing (or get a respirator).

1) Return of the repressed from youthful rebellion days. A throwback to the unquestioning obedience of authority which brought us a) WWI & WWII (right), and b) the fervour of the Internationale (left)

2) A last-chance grasping for Holy Unification that replicates the Feeling (but not the substance) of:
a) One Nation Under God;
b) One Generation of StarDust in pursuit of Common Goals

support the rich in the beurre-steak-and-ale style they are accustomed to. And to obey Daylight Savings time for God alone knows what reason. Something somebody said about the state of Denmark. Hmm, now, what was it....?

If the Age of Aquarius (ca 1879) is about individuals deviating from the herd response and the Land of Liberty hosts a tamanous orientation, then why is it so still easy to align people like iron filings on paper held over a magnet with just a few strokes of brass-faced media nibs dipped in money-ink? comments.title: Hedging my bets
comments.content:
Hopefully this isn’t excessively long…

I’ve had an idea in my head for a while to plant hedges as fences on my farm. I finally decided this was the year to do it, procured seed for a few different types of trees and made plans to sprout them. The plan was to get them started in the garden then plant them in their final position once they could out compete grasses and whatnot.

So I did a reading regarding growing my trees from seed. I’ve always had issues growing from seed so it was my main concern.
I got:
J: Colled
LW: Ffordd
RW: Elw

I took that as a no. I was a little surprised by that so I asked if I would be better off not trying to plant trees from seed in the garden.
I got:
J: Bendeth Fach
LW: Gwyn
RW: Lllosgwrn y Ddraig

I took that as a yes and as a hint to seek help. I asked if I would have better success getting someone else to grow my seedlings. I know plenty of green fingered folks that would be happy to do it and it would perhaps get me a little more of a guarantee for success.
I got:
J: Colled
LW: Mab
RW: Pen y Ddraig

I took that as a no. I’ll speed things up. I asked if I should even plant trees as hedges: Colled. I asked is my hedge idea a good idea: Colled. I got irritated and asked should I just abandon this idea and I got Ffordd four times in a row and stopped the reading.

I asked if I should choose different trees than the ones I obtained seed for and got:
J: Cyswllt
LW: Carchar
RW: Ffordd

I didn’t know how to take that one. It seemed favorable so I asked about a specific type of tree that is native, grows abundantly on my property and is a typical hedge plant where hedges are used. I got colled… again.

So I decided to get really simple. Should I plant a hedge? Cyswllt.

Am I doing something wrong? The repeated colled result seems like a hint I'm not taking. I feel like if I keep pursuing this line of questioning I’m going to start irritating the oracle (and no doubt try your patience with an endless wall of text). Have my interpretations been off? In the past when I got a yes to a question it was a clear yes but with this I feel like I’m working through a flow chart that keeps sending me back to the beginning. Help?

replies.title: Re: Hedging my bets
replies.content: Sigh.  Kinda had a feeling.  It's worth noting that I don't have any other examples of hedges in the style of England and Ireland in the US.  Perhaps there's a reason.  Anybody out there have any theories?  They seem to be such a pragmatic solution to the permanent problem of fencing on the farm.

replies.title: Re: Hedging my bets
replies.content: Do you know how they make/made real hedges in Europe? Its not just a matter of planting trees, you have to let them grow to a certain height, then cut them and bend them, so they grow together.  It seems to take a certain amount of skill. There are some YouTube videos on it so that you can see how it's done, but really, it'd  be much easier and faster to just do it with metal wire fencing and posts (of black locust if you can.) It would take qhite a number of years to get a hedge to the point that it would stop animals.

replies.title: Re: Hedging my bets
replies.content:
Years ago I came across an old US Dept of Agriculture pamphlet on making literal hedge fences from the hedge tree also known as Osage Orange. It's native to the Midwest and there is no better firewood, burns hot enough to melt a stove pipe if you're careless.

There were thousands of hedgerows planted here in the Midwest after the dust bowl years, as a conservation measure. The pamphlet (which was lost on some old hard drive but maybe still findable on the interwebs) even calculated the cost per foot to hire some guys to dig the trenches.

The instructions (in case you get a favorable reading at some point) were to freeze the balls over winter to free the seed, then plant them every 6 inches in a shallow trench. Once the saplings are a few feet tall you bend and weave every fifth one diagonally through the other four, so they look like counting tallies. Keep the side sprouts trimmed and in a few years you have a fence tight enough a rabbit would have trouble squeezing through and strong enough to contain a bull. All the trimmings are a plus for winter firewood.

I've wanted to try it for years since the horses invariably pull the electric fence down, so finally last fall I collected a 5 gallon bucket full of hedgeballs. They are now a seedy mush waiting for a warm day. 🌱

comments.title: Clairvoyance follow-up question
comments.content:
Last week I asked about symbols I see, and you said it indicated a talent for clairvoyance. Could you say more about what clairvoyance is and how it works, how it could be used?

Someone else asked about seeing scenes and you called it hypnogogic imagery. Is that basically the same thing as clairvoyance, or how is it different?

Thanks; your answer last week was very encouraging and really kind of cool.

replies.title: Re: Clairvoyance follow-up question
replies.content:
There are several different kinds of clairvoyance; the one you probably have is the ability to see things on the astral plane -- one of the subtle planes of being, the realm of dream, imagination, and concrete consciousness. Each of us has sense organs on each of the planes where we have fully developed bodies -- the material plane of dense matter, the etheric plane of life force, and the astral plane of concrete consciousness -- and part of the process of occult training involves learning to awaken and use the sense organs on the etheric and astral planes. You've basically got a head start on the process, probably because you did some work developing your astral vision in a previous life.

As for what you can do with it, that depends on subtle details that will become clear over time if you take up occult practice. Some people with that gift find that they can use it to see other people's auras -- the aura is the outer layer of the astral body, and if you can see it clearly you can perceive what's going on in their emotions, and also sometimes diagnose health problems. Other people find that their gift is helpful for interacting with spirits or for perceiving the same sorts of patterns of events about to happen that astrologers track.

Hypnagogic imagery is sometimes but not always clairvoyant in nature. The label simply refers to any spontaneous imagery you see as you're going to sleep; the equivalent seen as you wake up is hypnopompic imagery.

All that democracy requires is the recognition that a community thrives best if it grants its members equality under the law. Human beings aren't equal in any other sense -- no one of us is exactly equal to any other, a point C.S. That's democracy. Now of course you can also get there from monotheism, but it's an embarrassing display of bad faith for Chesterton to quietly erase the historical origins of democracy in the western world in yet another attempt to cram his doctrines down other people's throats.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Does the occult philosophy have a solution for the Fermi Paradox?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Fermi Paradox isn't a paradox at all. It's a proof that limitless technological progress is impossible. Set it out in a syllogism:

Major Premise: If limitless technological progress were possible, then we would see evidence of advanced extraterrestrial civilizations elsewhere in space.

Minor Premise: We do not see evidence of advanced extraterrestrial civilizations elsewhere in space.

Conclusion: Therefore, limitless technological progress is impossible.

The only reason this isn't obvious to most people is that our culture has blind faith in the possibility of limitless technological progress, so people flail around for ways to justify that faith and avoid noticing what's right in front of their faces, for all the world like Creationists trying to scrape together reasons to keep believing that the world was made in 4004 BC.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Their limitless technological progress, being limitless, has allowed them to hide all evidence of their existence from us.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Using exactly the same logic, I can prove that there's a small shoggoth perched on top of your head at this moment. It's a special kind of shoggoth, you see -- invisible, intangible, odorless, and weightless. Prove me wrong!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I can’t prove you’re wrong because you’re right. She’s been there for years. Actually I quite enjoy her company , except when she drops cheese-polenta crumbs in my hair.

You’re making the same point I was making. This Fermi paradox is insoluble unless you set limits at the start.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Fermi Paradox is only a paradox if all its premises are true. The obvious "solution" to the "paradox" is that one or more of its premises are false, and by far the most likely candidates are those premises which assume without evidence that interstellar travel can be made possible and viable.

Solutions that try to reconcile all the premises by tacking on further premises, like the premise that all advanced alien civilizations are purposefully hiding their existence from us, are also possible but far less plausible because they rely on more unproven assumptions.

replies.title: Re: Enhancing Memory
replies.content:
Not related to magic, but this reminded me of an exercise from Dominic O' Brien's memory training. In his books on mnemonic techniques, he includes some exercises like these to experiment with different facets of memory.

The steps he laid out for this one ("Time Travel")

- Choose some specific starting point from your past, like a school playground, a museum, and attic, a corner of a garden, and try to picture little details of the space involved

- Try to recall people connected to this place, their voices, the way they laughed, certain mannerisms

- Try to recapture the sounds you once heard in this place, a squeaky door, a train that would pass by, children playing outside, music that played at the time. Then smells, like soil, fresh flowers, polished wood. Then how the surroundings felt to touch, stone walls, iron gates, fabric coverings, etc.

- Try to remember your emotional state at the time: generally happy, melancholy, carefree, unsure, in love, etc.

See what memories arise as you choose different spaces from your past, and focus on them through the sense impressions that you had.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy and Lesson Plans
replies.content: Okay, let's review the basics. In a house chart, if you have a mode of perfection connecting the significators, the answer is yes; if not, the answer is no. Do you have a mode of perfection connecting the significators? Yes, Populus is your significator and that of your son, and it springs to the 8th house, into conjunction with the 9th, the significator of Thor. Meanwhile Thor's significator, Conjunctio, springs to the 2nd and 6th houses, into conjunction with your significator and that of your son. Meanwhile, Rubeus -- what color is Thor's beard? -- makes a translation by appearing in the 10th house (next to Thor's significator) and the 12th house (next to your significator). That is to say, you've got three different ways of saying "YES!"  If you see that as not especially positive or negative, all I can say is that you're a difficult person to please...

comments.title: Safety precautions with memory methods

Hi JMG,

I have a couple of questions about memory methods.

I've been reading 'Memory Craft' by Lynne Kelly and working out some exercises based on it. By the way, I could wholeheartedly recommend this book to anyone who is looking for an extremely accessible and practical introduction into a variety of memorisation techniques. It includes the classical memory palaces but also there is a good number of techniques from oral and semi literate cultures that Lynne has studied, tried out, and presented in her book. All with numerous lively examples of how one would approach using these techniques to memorise information.

So trying on some of these methods I feel a bit strange about some of them. So here are the questions:

1. One of the methods is using one's body as a memory palace. Every part of the body could be assigned to memorise a particular concept through usual means - colourful characters that stand out enough to be memorable on their own but also engaged into activities that are hard to forget. The usual guidelines is to put something as violent, sexual, outrageous, disgusting, or all of those combined as possible - the stronger the emotional response the better. For me, placing these images around my body would be an excellent way to memorise - visualising for me is more like tactilising (or kinaestethising...) and body awareness exercises were always easy for me generally speaking. However, I don't think I would feel good about placing some images associated with strong emotions into my body. I can imagine that remembering the 7 deadly sins by placing images of them around one's body and reviving them from time to time to keep the memory alive could produce some rather unpleasant results. Let's say I memorise the same 7 deadly sins by walking around my neighbourhood and visualising, as vividly as possible, emotionally disturbing examples of these sins in people's front yards. It would also be possible to memorise the events of the Pathworkings and the meditations after them using this palace. As a side note for those interested in obtaining this, I was unable to find Camphora 10M anywhere so far. The Indian pharmacies are probably the best bet right now. The 1M is available from Urenus, which sells on Amazon. Everywhere else I've looked is not currently stocking it.

Helios in the UK may stock it--they usually have literally everything--but I couldn't find it on their website.

In a real pinch, ANY potency of Camphora could help. Put the pellet in 4oz water, and give half a teaspoon as often as needed until there's a notable response. You can see how often that is, and continue that pattern for awhile to avoid symptoms returning in the interim. For example, if symptoms return or get worse toward the end of 2 hours, give a dose every 90 minutes. You can repeat every 5 minutes if necessary. It's much harder to overdo dosing and cause an aggravation in an acute illness, especially severe illness, which by definition is a violent reaction by the life force.

Be sure to stir, or tap the glass on a book firmly (a canning jars with a lid works well) before each dose. That is thought to prevent the vital force adapting to the remedy, causing it to lose some efficacy. Keep it in the fridge or add a dash of high proof booze to keep the water from souring.

A few extra tips, try to avoid treating fevers below 104°F unless there's a medical reason, as the fever itself is a great antiviral. For every degree of temperature elevation you have to increase fluid intake by at least 10% per degree. If there is sweating, double that and don't forget to add a pinch of salt. Gatorade and the like should be diluted 50%. None of this is meant as "medical advice", just common sense info every parent knows hopefully! (What I used to tell my patients before quitting allopathy).

There's anecdotal evidence, currently being tested, that chloroquine might help. Tonic water isn't what it used to be, but hey, maybe an excuse for a prophylactic G&T :d Would NOT recommend this for anyone already ill, since you don't want to be taxing your liver when acutely ill.
